{{Short description="Law that the direction of an induced current opposes the change causing it"}}
{{Infobox physical law
| name = Lenz's law
| image = Lenzs-law-inductor.jpg
| caption = A magnet falling through a copper tube falls slower than gravity alone, as eddy currents induced according to Lenz's law oppose the motion.
| formula = The negative sign in <math>\mathcal{E} = -\frac{d\Phi_B}{dt}</math>
| discovered_by = [[Heinrich Lenz]] (1834)
}}

'''Lenz's law''', named after the physicist [[Heinrich Lenz]] who formulated it in 1834, states that the direction of an [[electric current|induced current]] in a conductor by a changing [[magnetic field]] is such that the [[magnetic field]] created by the induced current opposes the initial changing magnetic field. It is a qualitative law that specifies the direction of induced current, but states nothing about its magnitude.

Lenz's law is encapsulated in the negative sign in [[Faraday's law of induction]]:
:<math>\mathcal{E} = -\frac{d\Phi_B}{dt}</math>

== Statement ==
:''The current induced in a circuit due to a change in magnetic flux is so directed as to oppose the change in flux that produces it.''

== Physical basis ==
Lenz's law is a consequence of the [[conservation of energy]]. If the induced current reinforced the change (rather than opposed it), a small change would trigger a positive feedback loop, creating unlimited energy — a violation of the [[first law of thermodynamics]].

== Examples ==

=== Magnet and coil ===
When the north pole of a magnet is pushed into a coil:
* Magnetic flux through the coil increases
* Induced current flows to create a magnetic field opposing the increase
* The induced field repels the approaching magnet

When the magnet is pulled out:
* Flux decreases
* Induced current reverses to create a field supporting the decreasing flux
* The induced field attracts the receding magnet

=== Falling magnet in a copper tube ===
A magnet dropped through a vertical copper tube falls slower than in free fall. The changing flux induces [[eddy current]]s in the tube, which create magnetic fields opposing the magnet's motion (magnetic braking).

=== Eddy current braking ===
{{Main|Eddy current brake}}
Eddy current brakes use Lenz's law for contactless braking in:
* Roller coasters
* High-speed trains
* Exercise equipment

== Applications ==
* [[Induction heating]] — induced eddy currents generate heat
* [[Electromagnetic braking]]
* [[Metal detector]]s
* [[Induction motor]]s
* [[Magnetic levitation]]
* [[Transformer]] operation — induced secondary current opposes the flux change

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Faraday's law of induction]]
* [[Eddy current]]
* [[Electromagnetic induction]]
* [[Right-hand rule]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Lenz's law| ]]
[[Category:Electromagnetism]]
[[Category:Scientific laws]]
